name: Howlands Landing (Catalina Island)
short_description: Collect chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens on this scenic BLM beach on Catalina Island. Easy coastal rockhounding with stunning island geology and ocean views.
description:
Howlands Landing on Catalina Island offers rockhounders a unique opportunity to collect specimens along a pristine Southern California coastal zone. Located on BLM-managed land at sea level, this spot combines accessible beach collecting with the distinctive mineralogy of the Channel Islands, an area rich in altered volcanic and metamorphic rocks. The landing sits in a geologically active zone where Tertiary-age volcanic and sedimentary rocks have been uplifted and weathered, creating abundant chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens that wash onto the beach with regular tide action.

The terrain at Howlands Landing is exceptionally flat and easy to navigate, making it ideal for families and beginner rockhounders. The beach access provides consistent exposure to new material as waves work weathered outcrops and scattered deposits. You will find specimens in a range of colors and formations, from banded chalcedony to opaque jasper with interesting patterns. The island setting means you will need to plan a boat trip to reach this location, but the effort rewards you with specimens that reflect Catalina's unique geologic history.

Collecting here is governed by standard BLM regulations allowing personal use only. The flat topography and beach setting mean you can cover significant ground without strenuous hiking. Bring a bucket, hand tools, and screen for sorting through beach gravels. The protected island environment and consistent wave action mean fresh material appears regularly, so repeat visits often yield new finds. Early morning collecting before boat traffic peaks is ideal for a peaceful experience.
history: Catalina Island sits within the Peninsular Ranges province and represents a fragment of Tertiary-age submarine volcanic and sedimentary rocks that have been uplifted and exposed by regional tectonics. The chalcedony and jasper found at Howlands Landing formed as silica-rich fluids altered volcanic rocks during the Tertiary period, roughly 30-65 million years ago. This same geologic process created the diverse mineral deposits scattered throughout the Channel Islands.
tips: Search the active beach zone below the high-tide line where wave action concentrates heavier mineral specimens. Look for pieces of banded chalcedony and opaque jasper in shades of red, tan, gray, and cream. Bring a 1/4-inch screen to sift beach gravels and sand for smaller polishable specimens, and use a small pry bar to check loose rocks in the beach face for specimens. The best material often collects in depressions and along debris lines after significant wave action.
terrain_notes: Elevation 105 feet in a flat, coastal setting. The terrain is essentially flat with easy walking along sandy and rocky beach zones. Footing is firm sand and stable pea gravel with some larger beach rocks scattered throughout. No significant elevation change. Distance from boat landing to productive collecting areas is typically less than half a mile. The coastal exposure means constant wind and salt spray.
best_season: April - October. This period offers the most stable weather for boat access to the island and comfortable conditions for beach collecting. Winter months bring increased wave action and storm surge that can limit access and beach stability. Summer temperatures on the beach are mild due to ocean influence.
written_directions: Howlands Landing is accessible only by private boat or commercial water taxi from Avalon or Two Harbors on Catalina Island. From Avalon harbor, navigate approximately 3 nautical miles northwest along the coast to reach Howlands Landing, recognizable by its distinctive sandy beach and rocky outcrops. The landing provides direct beach access suitable for small boats. If using Avalon as your base, arrange boat transportation through local marinas or water taxi services. There are no road access points to this location.
parking_notes: Beach landing area with boat access. No vehicle parking. Anchor in shallow water or use water taxi to reach the beach.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. This is BLM land managed for public recreation. All specimens collected must remain for personal use; resale is prohibited.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect tide schedules and water safety
nearest_services: Avalon, approximately 3 nautical miles southeast. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available in Avalon. Stock supplies before heading to Howlands Landing.
accessibility_notes: Flat, easy terrain with no elevation change makes this accessible to most hikers and families. Beach footing is stable sand and pea gravel with good traction. No wheelchair access to the beach due to boat-only access. Sturdy shoes recommended for rocky areas. The coastal exposure means wind and salt spray; bring sun protection and fresh water.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Howlands Landing on Catalina Island?
faq.answer: Howlands Landing is known for chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. You will find banded chalcedony in cream, tan, and gray tones, along with opaque jasper with interesting color patterns. The specimens range from small beach gravels to larger palm-sized pieces suitable for polishing or display. The island's Tertiary-age volcanic history created these silica-rich deposits.

faq.question: How do I get to Howlands Landing for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Howlands Landing is accessible only by water. From Avalon harbor, arrange boat transportation through local marinas or water taxi services. The location is approximately 3 nautical miles northwest along the coast. You can also use commercial water taxi services that operate from Avalon. Plan your trip around tide schedules for safe beach access.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Howlands Landing?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal rockhounding at Howlands Landing, which sits on BLM public land. You are limited to 25 lbs of specimens per day under FLPMA regulations. Collection is for personal use only; commercial collecting and resale are prohibited. This is a recreational collection zone with straightforward public access rules.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Howlands Landing?
faq.answer: April through October offers the most favorable conditions for boat access and beach collecting. Summer and early fall provide stable weather and comfortable temperatures. Winter months bring increased storm surge and wave action that can limit access. Spring offers excellent conditions with fresh material exposed by winter wave action.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring for collecting at Howlands Landing?
faq.answer: Bring a hand hammer or small pry bar, bucket, small shovel, and a 1/4-inch screen for sifting beach gravels. A collection bag, sun protection, and at least 2 liters of fresh water are essential. Wear sturdy shoes suitable for rocky terrain. The beach setting and coastal exposure mean wind protection and a hat are also recommended.

faq.question: Is Howlands Landing good for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Howlands Landing is excellent for beginners and families. The flat, easy terrain requires no hiking skills or climbing experience. Children ages 6 and up can participate comfortably. The beach setting makes it visually interesting and safer than remote inland sites. Specimens are accessible in beach gravels, requiring only basic sorting and collecting skills.

meta_description: Collect chalcedony and jasper at Howlands Landing on Catalina Island. BLM public beach access, no permit needed. Ideal for families and beginners.
